metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an automated proving command to AC2 that captures a baseline and asserts the reduction, e.g. store the pre-change count in a committed `.readme-line-baseline` file and add a check: `[ $(wc -l < README.md) -le $(($(cat .readme-line-baseline) - 40)) ] || echo 'CATALOG NOT REMOVED'`; or at minimum embed the before-count as a literal in the AC so the single-command check is self-contained.
findings.finding: AC2 has no inline automated proving command — it relies on a manual before/after `wc -l` comparison ('record in a comment') with no single command that fails if the threshold isn't met. An executor can satisfy this criterion by reporting numbers without the catalog actually being removed.
findings.suggested_fix: Store the pre-change line count in the plan/ticket as a literal (e.g. 'baseline: 312 lines') and add an inline command: `BEFORE=312; [ $(wc -l < README.md) -le $((BEFORE - 40)) ] || echo 'SHRINK THRESHOLD NOT MET'`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC3's heading loop to include at least one representative heading for the 'what/why' and 'routing' sections (e.g. `'## What is rebar'` or `'## Overview'` and `'## More'` / `'## Docs'`), or document that those sections have no fixed heading and will be verified by prose review.
findings.finding: The Scope section requires preserving 'what/why' narrative and 'routing' sections, but AC3 only verifies `## Install` and `## Quickstart` headings — the 'what/why' and 'routing' sections have no corresponding verification, leaving those scope-required sections unchecked.
findings.location: Scope bullet 2 vs. Acceptance Criteria — AC3

findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit Scope carve-out: the `mcp-name: io.github.navapbc/rebar` registry-ownership annotation in `## MCP server` must be preserved in the README (it is not a catalog entry — it is a machine-read ownership signal required in the PyPI long description).
findings.finding: The plan's mechanism for replacing the '## MCP server' section with a link to docs/mcp-reference.md risks inadvertently deleting the 'mcp-name: io.github.navapbc/rebar' registry ownership annotation, which the README itself explicitly states must be kept in the README because 'The registry verifies PyPI-package ownership via this annotation (kept in this README, which is the PyPI long description).' The plan's Scope and ACs have no preservation carve-out for this annotation, leaving an implementer who treats the whole section as a volatile catalog free to remove it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'Exception: the `mcp-name: io.github.navapbc/rebar` annotation in `## MCP server` is NOT catalog content — it is a machine-read registry ownership signal that must remain in README.md (the PyPI long description). Preserve it in the trimmed MCP pointer section.' Optionally add an AC: `grep -qF 'mcp-name: io.github.navapbc/rebar' README.md || echo 'MISSING MCP-NAME ANNOTATION'`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `docs/manifest-signing.md:62` as a consumer that holds an inbound anchor link `[Configuration](../README.md#configuration)` — either preserve the `## Configuration` heading/anchor, add a redirect stub, or update the link in manifest-signing.md as part of this ticket's edit set.
findings.finding: docs/manifest-signing.md line 62 holds an inbound anchor link `[Configuration](../README.md#configuration)` targeting the `## Configuration` section of README.md. That section (lines 548–583) contains a TOML config-key catalog with env names and precedence rules — exactly the type of volatile config catalog the plan intends to trim. If the executor removes or renames `## Configuration`, the inbound relative link from docs/manifest-signing.md breaks. The plan enumerates no consumers outside README.md and names only `README.md` in its Files list.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `docs/manifest-signing.md` to the Files list and include a step: if `## Configuration` is removed or renamed, update the cross-reference at `docs/manifest-signing.md:62` to point to `docs/config.md` (the full config reference the plan acknowledges already exists).

findings.finding: AC line 3 asserts 'No dead links (passes the docs link/index CI gate)' but no such docs link/index CI gate exists in the repository. A search across all CI workflow files (.github/workflows/test.yml, gerrit-verify.yaml, and all other workflows), the Makefile, and .pre-commit-config.yaml found no link-checker step (no lychee, no markdown-link-check, no link-index drift gate, no dead-link validation). Asserting that this AC is satisfied by passing a non-existent gate leaves it unverifiable and potentially silently unmet.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'No dead links (passes the docs link/index CI gate)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'passes the docs link/index CI gate' with a concrete, existing check — e.g. 'All URLs in README.md that point to generated reference pages resolve to real file paths on the `docs/` branch (verified by a new `make check-links` step or lychee CI step added as part of this work)'.

findings.finding: AC3 asserts 'passes the docs link/index CI gate' as a verification mechanism, but no such gate exists in the repo's CI workflows (test.yml and gerrit-verify.yaml were fully searched — neither contains a link-checking step).

AC3 asserts 'No dead links (passes the docs link/index CI gate)' as the verification mechanism, but no such gate exists in the codebase. Examining test.yml and all scripts shows the CI suite contains drift gates for env-vars, types, prompt-index, criteria-routing, and server manifest — none of which check markdown hyperlinks in README.md. The plan's no-dead-links guarantee relies on a gate that is either absent or undefined, leaving AC3 unenforceable as written.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'passes the docs link/index CI gate' in AC3 with either: (a) the name of a specific existing CI check that covers README.md markdown links, or (b) a concrete manual or scripted verification step (e.g. 'run `python scripts/check_readme_links.py` — to be added — and confirm exit 0'), and add that script/step to the Files section if it needs to be created.

findings.finding: AC3 ('No dead links — passes the docs link/index CI gate') asserts a CI gate exists to verify link validity, but no such docs link-checker gate is present in any .github/workflows/ file. The plan stakes its dead-link acceptance criterion on an unverified infrastructure capability.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'No dead links (passes the docs link/index CI gate)'
findings.suggested_fix: Either reference the specific ticket/step that creates the docs link-checker gate, or add a concrete fallback: 'manually verify all introduced links resolve to existing files before merging, pending a future link-check CI gate.'

findings.finding: AC2 uses the evaluative phrase 'stands alone as an intro' without a deterministic, binary check. 'Stands alone' is a human judgment that different reviewers will apply differently.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace 'The README still stands alone as an intro (what/install/quickstart/routing)' with: 'README.md retains dedicated sections for: project description, installation, quickstart, and routing links (verifiable by section-heading grep).'

Proving command: `grep -E '^#+ *(Install|Quick[Ss]tart|Rout)' README.md`

findings.finding: AC1 ('volatile catalogs are removed and replaced with links') and AC2 ('README still stands alone as an intro') have no stated proving command. Only AC3 has a concrete CI gate. Without a proving command for AC1, completion of the removal is asserted, not verified; AC2 has no deterministic check at all.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
